facts.text: Kew Gardens was founded in 1909 by lawyer Alrick Man, who imagined a “garden suburb” for New York’s emerging professional class.
The neighborhood’s main commercial spine, Lefferts Boulevard, grew around the stone‑and‑timber Kew Gardens LIRR Station, a landmark that anchors the community.
In 1920 the Kew Gardens Inn opened near the railroad station, offering guests a room, bath, and meals for $40 a week.
The Ralph Bunche House, home of the Nobel Peace Prize winner, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and is a National Historic Landmark.
Kew Gardens’ Forest Park Carousel, a historic carousel, was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2004.
The neighborhood’s name comes from the nearby Kew Gardens in London, reflecting the early 20th‑century garden suburb ideal.
